observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management thru the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer conduct the monthly reconciliation of cashbook and accounting records to ensure timely discovery of errors and to facilitate early preparation of applicable adjustments.

The management stated that the concerned personnel have acknowledged the recommendations and have committed to conducting monthly reconciliations of their cashbooks and records.

We recommended that the Liquidating Officer strictly follow the provisions of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume II as regards the maintenance of cashbook to ensure completeness and correctness thereof and to facilitate verification and reconciliation.

The Accountable Officer has assured compliance with the recommendations

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare the monthly Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) for checks at the end of each month in compliance with the above-cited regulation.

The Accountable Officer has expressed its adherence to the recommendations.

We recommended that the Accountable Officer continue the proper handling of government funds pursuant to regulations.

The management stated that the accountable officer has pledged to comply with proper monitoring and daily cash count practices as required for items in his custody. Additionally, the officer has committed to strictly adhering to the recommendations provided.

We recommended and reiterated that the management require:

the Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant and the General Service Officer with the complete description and segregation of road components for those with data on construction cost. For those not posted in the registry but without identified cost and those included in the RPCRNS but not in the registry, the fair value must be used in determining the cost of road components and thereafter shall provide the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
the Municipal Accountant to depreciate the carrying amount of existing roads and its components to reflect the asset in its used condition and accordingly adjust the amount recorded in the Road Network account supported by the RPCRNS, Registry Infrastructure for Roads and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost per registry. Subsequently, at the end of each year, a lapsing schedule for the computation of depreciation for each depreciable road component must be prepared as basis for recording depreciation expenses and the required Local Road Network Ledger Card for each road network shall likewise be maintained; and
the designated Supply Officer at the end or every accounting period to render a Report on Local Road Network using the pro-forma schedule in Annex A of same Circular and maintain local road inventory, Road Map and complete Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We commended management for the implementation of all the PPAs under the LGSF-SBDP of the NFT-ELCAC which has resulted in the optimum use of resources and the prompt delivery of benefits to the constituents of the LGU.

Nevertheless, we recommended that the BAC/BAC Secretariat immediately submit the lacking procurement documents for further evaluation by this office.

We recommended that management exert effort to locate the MOAs and to provide the same to the audit team for further evaluation. In case there are no MOAs, the LGU should inquire from the source agency about the purpose or deadlines for the implementation of PPAs.

We recommended that management the concerned Project Team to exert effort to comply with the MOA. Otherwise, the fund balance should be returned to the Bureau of Treasury.

It is necessary to determine the source of these fund transfers as to ascertain whether these are recorded in the appropriate account and to determine their specific purpose and the timelines involved for its implementation.

We recommended the Management to
conduct a comprehensive review of all relevant financial documents, including bank statements, transaction records, invoices, and receipts that might help identify the source of the transfers;
Examine records of completed projects, activities, and programs to determine if any correspond to the amounts transferred. Cross-reference these records with financial data to identify potential matches; and
Review and strengthen internal controls and procedures for fund transfers to prevent similar issues in the future.

Management has pledged to make efforts to obtain Memoranda of Agreement (MOAs) for project agreements. If MOAs are unavailable, the LGU will communicate with the source agency to clarify the required purpose and implementation timeline. Regarding funds with unidentified sources, management assures that it will assess documents to identify their origins and review other records to find possible matches.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs to establish the veracity of account balances in the financial statements; and
maintain subsidiary ledgers per Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and facilitate the monitoring of the existence of funds and the timely implementation thereof.

The management acknowledged the long outstanding balances with no related records found upon verification. However, the accounting office will conduct further verification of prior years' records to trace original entries for validation. Management pledges regular verification, analysis, and validation of Due to National Government Agencies (NGAs) and Due to Local Government Units (LGUs) to ensure accuracy in financial statements. Additionally, subsidiary ledgers of the NGAS will be maintained to ensure complete information on accounts, facilitating fund monitoring and timely implementation, as recommended.

We commended the management for its consistent adherence to Section 287 of R.A. No. 7160 by providing the required amount out of its IRA for development projects and compliance to DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2020 by including only eligible projects, all for the benefit its constituents.

We recommended that management:
prioritize and accelerate the implementation of its ongoing projects funded by the 20% MDF to ensure that the allocated funds are utilized efficiently and effectively and ensure timely delivery of benefits to the community, such as improved infrastructure, services, and economic opportunities;
continuously align development projects funded by the 20% Development Fund (DF) with the municipality's development goals and the needs of its constituents. Projects should be selected based on their potential to contribute to efficient and effective local governance, as well as their ability to promote the general welfare of the people; and
ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready to avoid delays, cost overruns, and other issues that may arise during project execution.

The Management informed the audit team that as of March 31, 2024, the status of implementation of the 20% Development Fund from the 2023 Current Appropriation indicates that 4 projects, previously ongoing as of December 31, 2023, have been completed. Additionally, 5 projects, previously categorized as "Not Started" as of December 31, 2023, are now ongoing, while 1 project has been abandoned. For the Continuing Appropriations, 1 ongoing project and 1 previously "Not Started" project as of December 31, 2023, have been completed. Furthermore, 2 projects previously categorized as "Not Started" as of December 31, 2023, are now ongoing, while 2 projects have been abandoned. The Municipal Development Council (MDC) and other relevant offices have been instructed to take necessary actions to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended that in case the LGU opts to realign its existing PPAs under the 20% DF, including the continuing appropriations from previous years' 20% DF, it should effect changes in its annual budget through supplemental budgets in accordance with Section 321 of the Code and Article 417 of its IRR, as amended by Administrative Order No. 47, S. 1993.

The Management has expressed its commitment to follow the recommendations.

We recommended and reiterated the following:
the Municipal Accountant conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
in the future, for transactions involving receivables, the Municipal Accountant should maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

Management stated that the Municipal Accountant will perform regular verification, analysis, and validation of receivables to identify debtors and ascertain collectability. If deemed uncollectible, management will request authorization to write off dormant receivables. Additionally, the municipal accountant committed the maintenance of subsidiary ledgers for upcoming receivables transactions to ensure complete information on accounts owed to the municipality.

We commended management for its remarkable effort in complying with Section 89 of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002. Nevertheless, we recommended that management:
continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

Management assured that it will continue to require the immediate settlement of remaining overdue cash advances and withhold the salaries of concerned officials and employees for the settlement of such cash advances. Furthermore, for future transactions, the municipal accountant committed to strictly comply with pertinent provisions regarding the granting, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that management further strengthen its internal control by instructing all concerned to properly accomplish all accounting and procurement forms by strictly following the instructions in the MNGAS for LGUs and procurement laws and regulations to ensure propriety and propriety regularity of its transactions.

Management has expressed its commitment to follow the recommendations.

We recommended that Management:
Conduct a comprehensive review of the current payroll system to identify and rectify flaws that allow for non-compliance. Consider upgrading to a more reliable payroll management system that automatically flags deductions exceeding the allowable limit; and
revise its loan approval policies to include mandatory checks of an employee’s net take-home pay before approval. It should ensure that no approved loan results in a net take-home pay below ₱5,000.00. Further, this policy should be clearly communicated to all employees and enforced strictly by those responsible for loan approvals.

We recommended that management:
require the concerned personnel to submit the lacking documents supporting the payments of terminal leave benefits;
develop and enforce a checklist based on COA Circular No. 2012-001 to ensure all necessary documents are collected and verified before processing terminal leave payments. This checklist should be adhered to strictly by the responsible personnel to prevent any omissions; and
conduct regular training sessions for the Accounting and Human Resources staff and other relevant personnel on the requirements of P.D. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001, and the Omnibus Rules on Leave to reinforce the importance of compliance and to update any changes in regulations or procedures.

We recommended that Management implement the following measures:
Notice to Proceed:
Ensure that all Notice to Proceed documents include the date of receipt by the contractor. In cases where the date is missing, consider the date of the Notice to Proceed itself as the date of receipt.
Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA):
Implement a policy requiring the preparation of two SWAs for each project: one as of the supposed end of the contract duration and another as of the actual date of project completion. This will facilitate the accurate computation of liquidated damages against the contractor.
Preparation of SWA:
Establish a process where the SWA is prepared by the contractor and subsequently validated and/or confirmed by the procuring entity. This will ensure the accuracy and reliability of the SWA.
Certificate of Completion and Certificate of Acceptance:
Enforce the separation of the Certificate of Acceptance from the Certificate of Completion in project documentation. Additionally, ensure that the Certificate of Acceptance is issued no earlier than one year from the project's completion date.
Suspension of Work and Extension of Contract Time:
Strengthen oversight to ensure that all instances of work suspensions/extensions are supported by necessary documentary requirements. Non-compliance should result in the invalidation of the contract extension and the imposition of applicable liquidated damages in case of unjustified delays.
Non-imposition of Liquidated Damages:
Review all project contracts to ensure that liquidated damages are properly imposed for delays in project completion. Enforce the provisions of the contract agreements and the procurement law to hold contractors accountable for delays.
Submission of Procurement Documents:
Implement a system to track the submission of all procurement documents and documentary requirements on advance payment, progress payment, and final payment of infrastructure projects. Ensure that all required documents are submitted promptly to avoid delays in audit and inspection of projects.
Require personnel involved in procurement and project monitoring to undergo training and capacity-building activities to enhance their understanding of requirements and procedures.

Management noted the audit observations and they assured compliance on each of these recommendations.

We recommended the Management to direct the BAC/BAC Secretariat to prepare and submit the APP, PMR and APCPI Results to the GPPB and comply with the posting requirements as required in R.A. No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 02-2020. Thereafter, please furnish the Office of the Auditor of a copy of each report.

The management stated that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and its Secretariat were directed to prepare and submit the Annual Procurement Plan, Procurement Management Plan, and the Agency Procurement Compliance and Performance Indicator Results to the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB). The BAC Secretariat was specifically instructed to provide a copy of each of these reports to the auditor's office.

We recommended that within a period not exceeding fifteen (15) calendar days from the determination by the BAC of the bidder with the LCRB, HRRB, SCRB, or SRRB, and the recommendation to award the contract, the HoPE or his duly authorized representative shall approve or disapprove the said recommendation and immediately issue the Notice of Award to ensure the early implementation of projects.

We recommended the BAC/BAC Secretariat to strictly observe Section 37.4 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 and ensure that the Notice to Proceed are issued and received by the successful bidders within seven (7) calendar days from the date of contract approval.

We recommended the TWG and BAC to ensure that contracts be awarded only to bidders who are responsive to the project's requirements and evaluate the bids offered per pertinent provisions of Section 32 of 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184.

Management has declared its intention to comply with the recommendations.

We encourage Management to continuously ensure strict compliance with the revised policies, rules and procedures on the preparation of the Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) prescribed under Department Order (DO) No. 197, Series of 2016 and ensure proper observance of the provisions of the procurement law on conduct of pre-procurement, pre-bid and detailed evaluation of bids.

Management has expressed its adherence to the recommendations.

We recommended that:
the Local Chief Executive require the BAC/BAC Secretariat to immediately submit the documents lacking. Otherwise, we will be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension pursuant to COA rules and regulations;
to ensure that all lacking documents are submitted, the BAC Secretariat should accomplish the documentary requirements checklist and put index tabs/tabbings on the documents to be submitted; and
the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure that all supporting contract documents are complete in compliance with pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR, and submitted within five (5) days from execution of contracts in accordance with Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

Management has expressed its commitment to follow the recommendations and committed to submit the lacking documents to the COA office.

We recommended that:
the Local Chief Executive require the BAC/BAC Secretariat to immediately submit the documents lacking. Otherwise, we will be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension pursuant to COA rules and regulations;
to ensure that all lacking documents are submitted, the BAC Secretariat should accomplish the documentary requirements checklist and put index tabs/tabbings on the documents to be submitted; and
the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure that all supporting contract documents are complete in compliance with pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR, and submitted within five (5) days from execution of contracts in accordance with Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

Management has expressed its commitment to follow the recommendations and committed to submit the lacking documents to the COA office.

We reiterated our recommendation that management should reassess its practice of providing financial assistance to other LGUs, particularly those of higher income classification. Management should consider engaging in discussions with other LGUs in the Province to evaluate the practice and, if deemed necessary, discontinue it. This would help ensure that resources are utilized efficiently and effectively, in line with the principles of good governance and sound financial management.

Management acknowledged the recommendations and committed to reassess the practice of providing financial assistance to other Local Government Units (LGUs). They will engage in discussions with other LGUs to consider discontinuing this practice if deemed necessary.

We commended the Accounting Office for diligently recording transactions using the appropriate accounts, thereby ensuring the fair presentation of financial statements, particularly with regard to Trust Fund-PRDP. Henceforth, we recommended that the Accounting Office continuously observe the use of proper accounts in recording all current and future transactions.

We commended Management for its very efficient implementation of the PRDP-funded sub-project, as we encourage them to continue its close monitoring of the progress of the project to ensure that the accomplishment achieved thus far is sustained. Regular evaluations should be consistently carried out to track progress against milestones and to identify any potential deviations from the project plan.
We recommended that the Accountable Officer continue properly handling government funds pursuant to regulations.

observations.recommendation:
1.19. We recommended the following:
1.19.1. That the City Accountant draw the following journal entries:
• to recognize Construction-in-Progress for the projects that
were not yet completed as of December 31, 2022, and to
recognize the trust liability account that was reversed upon
payment:
Construction in Progress 64,613,357.89
Due to Other NGAs 64,613,357.89
• to recognize purchased PPE items which were partially paid
and delivered as of December 31, 2022, and to recognize the
trust liability account that was reversed upon payment:
Appropriate PPE accounts 93,760,428.33
Due to NGAs 93,760,428.33
• to recognize the cost of the completed project
“Construction of Barangay Building/Hall at Bakakeng
Central Barangay”:
Other Public Infrastructures 56,000,000.00
Prior Period Adjustment 56,000,000.00
1.19.2. That the City execute a Deed of Transfer to Barangay Bakakeng
Central for the formal handover of the completed project
“Construction of Barangay Building/Hall at Bakakeng Central
Barangay”, emphasizing therein that the barangay shall commit to
fund the cost of maintenance and repairs as provided for in the
City Government’s Project MOA with the DPWH. A corresponding
journal entry voucher shall also be transferred to the barangay for
their basis in recognizing the asset in their books.

We recommended and management agreed to ensure that all PPAs, as
appropriated, will have a detailed breakdown of its expenditures, and
henceforth, ensure that all transactions/expenditures of the City are
properly classified and recorded in accordance with the Revised Chart of
Accounts for LGUs.

We recommended that the City Government:
3.9.1. through the City Accountant, recognize the depreciation expenses
of the unrecorded assets and to take up the necessary adjusting
journal entries in the books of accounts to adjust the overstated
net book value of the PPE and Government Equity accounts,
respectively; and
3.9.2. henceforth, comply with the abovementioned relevant provisions
on deprecation, and periodically monitor the charging of
depreciation expense to avoid misstatements and to reflect the
true/accurate balances of PPE accounts.

We recommended the following:
4.8.1. the General Services Office prepare the Acceptance and
Inspection Report upon receipt of the delivered PPEs, and furnish
copies thereof to the Accounting Office right after the acceptance
of items; and
4.8.2. the City Accounting Office recognize in the books of accounts the
unrecorded PPE items and the corresponding effects in the
Accounts Payable and Government Equity accounts; and
henceforth, record transactions at the time goods are accepted,
not on the time the payment has been made.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the City Accounting
Office coordinate with the City Engineering Office and City Buildings and
Architecture Office for the determination of the costs of
replaced/removed materials on account of the repairs, renovations, and
rehabilitations made and to eventually record the derecognition of
determined values. Likewise, we recommend that Management review
the useful life of the repaired/ improved assets, whether the useful life
have been extended as a result of the expenditure, for the computation
of new monthly depreciation.

We recommended that Management through the City Accounting Office:
a. identify and rectify the discrepancies noted in the Accumulated
Depreciation balances and dates of acquisition; and
b. ensure that the necessary disclosures are provided in the Notes
to the FS in accordance with the IPSAS.

We recommended and the City Government agreed to:
7.11.1. thru the General Services Office, immediately prepare the
Acceptance and Inspection Report upon receipt of the delivered
goods and services, and furnish copies thereof to the
Accounting Office right after the acceptance of items;
7.11.2. thereafter, thru the Accounting Office, record in the books of
accounts the expenditures and the corresponding liability
account not yet recognized for a total of ₱37,336,351.16; and
henceforth, recognize transactions in the periods to which they
relate in order to faithfully adhere with the accrual basis of
accounting and matching principle.
7.11.3. formulate guidelines and procedures to enhance the proper
disposition and treatment of unpaid obligations and to enhance
the procurement process so that the obligation of transactions
incurred are implemented on the same year.
7.11.4. ensure that obligations are supported with valid claims as
evidenced by signed contracts, POs, MOA or equivalent
documents.

8.13. We recommended and Management agreed to:
8.13.1. install a system of procedures at the City HSO that would
ensure that every drug, medicine, vaccine or medical supply
donated to the City Government is properly received,
accounted, issued, utilized/distributed and reported on, and
that accountability over said items is established;
8.13.2. require the City HSO to furnish the CAO copies of the Property
Transfer Reports, inventory report and other documents
pertaining to the receipt and utilization/distribution of all
donated/transferred COVID-19 vaccines and ancillary supplies
to facilitate timely recording; and
8.13.3. require the CAO to prepare adjusting journal entry as
presented hereunder, to record the receipt and
issuance/distribution of COVID-19 vaccines and Ancillary
Supplies (with cost) and to recognize the remaining/unused
balance of the inventory items in the books of accounts:
Drugs and Medicine
2,498,344.72
Inventory
Medical, Dental and
Laboratory Supplies 68,021.35
Inventory
Prior Period Adjustment
(Drugs and Medicine
Expenses and Medical, 23,370,593.26
Dental and Laboratory
Supplies Expenses)
Prior Period Adjustment 25,936,959.33
8.13.4. as to the 40,669 vials COVID-19 vaccines and 119,810 ancillary
supplies received through donation with no costs/amount,
require the CAO to prepare the same adjusting journal entry to
record the receipt and issuance thereof, and to recognize the
remaining/unused balance of the inventory items in the books
of accounts. The fair value as at the date of acquisition/receipt
shall be the basis for recording.

We recommended and management agreed to:
9.15.1. recognize accrued income for the uncollected rental fees of
₱7,270,772.23 and deferred income for the advance payment of
₱892,470.00 on the lease of lots at Burnham Park and other lots
owned and administered by the City Government, and to strictly
adhere to the accrual basis of accounting in recognizing income
from all sources as prescribed under the International Public
Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS); and
9.15.2. require the City Treasury Office to periodically provide the City
Accounting Office with the list and details of all leaseholders
specifying those with delinquencies, those with advance
payments, including the amounts due such as unpaid rents,
penalties, surcharges and interests for the proper recording and
reporting in the books and in the Financial Statements.

We recommended and management agreed that , henceforth, the City
Accountant will prepare the FS of the City Government where: a) the
beginning balances of each SFPo and SFPer account affected by prior
period error are adjusted on a per line item basis; b) the amount of the
Government Equity in the SFPo reconciles with the restated beginning
balance for Equity in the SCNAE; and c) the Notes to FS provide
disclosure on the breakdown and nature of the prior period errors and
other adjustments, as well as the amount of correction for each FS line
item affected.

We recommended and management agreed that, henceforth, the City
Accountant comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS
particularly on the material accounts and significant changes to ensure
that relevant, reliable, comparable and understandable information is
available to the intended users of the Financial Statements.

We recommended and management agreed to:
12.9.1. Conduct a thorough re-evaluation of the 347 unimplemented
PPAs to determine whether the expected outputs remain
relevant and responsive to current needs of the local
constituents;
12.9.2. Fast-track the completion of those partially implemented and on-
going projects and immediately implement those PPAs that
remain to be a priority concern. The PPAs already considered
irrelevant and non-responsive to present circumstances be re-
appropriated to current and relevant interventions so as to
enhance the timely and positive delivery of public services; and
12.9.3. Ensure that the delivery of socio-economic services is within
pace with time-frames set by constantly monitoring the extent of
implementation of the PPAs identified and funded under the 20%
Local Development Plan and Budget by requiring:
12.9.3.1. The Local Finance Committee (LFC) to conduct a
regular review and general examination of cost and
accomplishments against performance standards
applied in undertaking development projects and
furnish a copy of such to the City Mayor and the
Sangguniang Panlungsod, pursuant to Section 316(h)
of the Local Government Code;
12.9.3.2. The Planning and Development Coordinator to monitor
and evaluate the implementation of the different
programs and activities of the city in accordance with
the approved development plan, pursuant to Section
476(b-4) of the Local Government Code; and
12.9.3.3. The Planning and Development Coordinator to prepare
and submit periodic reports to the City Mayor on the
status of implementation of the development plan and
incorporating therein issues and concerns affecting the
implementation, as basis of the Local Chief Executive
to take appropriate action.

We recommended and management agreed to:
13.7.1. Closely monitor and ensure that all projects being implemented
by the City are proceeding according to the plans and
specifications and that same are on schedule. If necessary,
issue notice of termination once a contractor incurs negative
slippage of more than 15%;
13.7.2. Impose the corresponding liquidated damages for the lapsed
projects once the concerned contractors make their
progress/final billings; and
13.7.3. Consider imposing other penalties to the erring contractors as
may be warranted under existing laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended and management agreed to religiously plan and
carryout detailed engineering investigations and other activities
required under Section 17.6 of the revised IRR of RA 9184 to ferret out
and settle all possible issues in order to ensure that the priority
development projects funded out of the 20% development fund are
procurement and implementation ready.

Further, we recommended and management agreed to institute a
calibrated plan of action to fast tract the implementation of the said 40
priority development projects.

We recommended that:
15.18.1. the City Government execute an Amended Project MOA with
the DPWH whereby the latter explicitly approves of the
“Clustering” of the DPWH Project “Multi-Purpose Building,
Athletic Bowl Oval, Burnham Park, Baguio City” with the City
Government Project “Baguio Youth Convergence Center and
Indoor Sports Complex, Athletic Bowl, Burnham Park” in
conformity to the following provision of DPWH Department
Order No. 62, s. 2018, re: Revised Guidelines for the
Implementation of DPWH Projects (by Administration of
Contract) by LGUs:
“Section 4. Project Memorandum of Agreement-
4.1 Every Project to be undertaken by an LGU in
accordance with the provisions of this Department Order
shall be covered by a Project MOA to be entered into
between the DPWH and the LGU concerned using the format
in Annexes “C and D”.
4.2 The DPWH officials authorized to enter and approve the
said MOA shall be in accordance with the latest Levels of
Authorities and Officials of the DPWH for Procurement.” `
15.18.2. Else, the City Government fulfill the terms of the original
Project MOA with the DPWH in undertaking Implementation by
Contract of the Project “Construction of Multi-Purpose
Building, Athletic Bowl Oval, Burnham Park, Baguio City”
amounting to ₱50,000,000.00, which means undertaking
separate procurement procedures for the project as required
under Section 2.1 of subject MOA, quoted as follows:
“The LGU shall undertake the procurement for the project
through Competitive Bidding in accordance with RA 9184
and its Implementing Rules and Regulations”

We recommended and management agreed to:
16.11.1. Consider adopting the procedures for inspection and
assessment as well as the collection of payments for
Arrival/Entrance Fees as implemented in the City Public Market
through Memorandum Order No. 01-22 dated July 27, 2022.
Proper segregation of functions in the inspection, assessment
and collection of payments for Arrival/Entrance Fees in the
City Abattoir/Slaughter Area and other collection points
should be put in place;
16.11.2. Consider deploying more computer machines within the
various collection points and discontinue the use of manual or
handwritten method in the accomplishment of Official
Receipts in order to minimize error and/or to eliminate the
possibility of tampering of receipts; and
16.11.3. Ensure that details of the amounts being collected are properly
stated in the Official Receipts and that all ORs are duly
supported with assessment documents such as copies of the
Goods Delivery Declaration Forms and Inspection Reports.

We recommended the following:
17.8.1. the City Treasury Office regularly submit a Certified List of
Taxpayers and Tax Due based on the RPTAR/ RPTIC to City
Accounting Office and use it as basis for the setting up of
RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year pursuant to
Section 20 of the NGAS Manual;
17.8.2. the City Accounting Office and City Treasury Office reconcile
the discrepancies between the RPT/SET Receivable accounts
and the List of Real Property Tax delinquencies to facilitate the
necessary adjustments in the books of accounts; and
17.8.3. the City Assessor continue to clean and update the RPTACS
being that their records are the primary source of data on real
property taxes.

We recommended that the City Government:
18.7.1. strengthen and intensify the collection system and tax campaign
to eliminate and/or minimize tax delinquencies; and
18.7.2. institute measures to update the Assessment Roll such as
reassessment of real properties due to partial or total destruction
or due to a major change in the actual use, among others, in order
to come up with an updated computation with the correct and
realistic real property taxes to be collected.

We recommended and management agreed to expedite and ensure the
full implementation of the Programs, Projects and Activities as planned
in order to fully achieve the objectives, goals and targets of the City on
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Program.

We recommended and management agreed that the LDRRMC review
and evaluate the City’s future LDRRMFIP with emphasis on the proper
identification and selection of specific PPAs that is to be given priority
and focus. Also, ensure that the CDRRMO fully include in the
LDRRMFIP the PPAs to be funded both by the Current Appropriations
and the Special Trust Fund unexpended balances of previous years,
continuing appropriations, including those financial assistance/grants
received from the national government, so as to facilitate the
monitoring of fund utilization.

We recommended and Management agreed, that the CDRRMO strictly
comply with the reporting requirements stipulated in RA 10121 and
COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 2012 to ensure that financial
information are appropriately reported and timely disclosed.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
22.15.1. Institutionalize the GAD Database to have an effective basis for
gender-responsive planning, programming and policy
formulation;
22.15.2. Create a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team to assess and
monitor the City’s GAD policies, programs and projects;
22.15.3. Create / assign a Responsibility Center and code for GAD and
use it to prepare the reports for GAD-related expenses and
other GAD-related financial transactions prescribed in Item
4.1.C.8 and Item 7.0 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Nos.
2013-01 and 2016-01, respectively; and
22.15.4. henceforth, furnish the Audit Team with copies of Annual GPB
and GAD AR within the period prescribed.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management exhaust all
available remedies provided under the laws, rules and regulations in
enforcing immediate compliance for the settlement of the Charges and
for the Disallowances without pending appeal to COA as provided in
the RRSA.
We recommended that the Accountable Officer continue properly handling government funds pursuant to regulations.

We recommended that management require the Accounting and Treasury Offices to conduct monthly reconciliation of cashbook and accounting records to ensure timely discovery of errors and to facilitate early preparation of applicable adjustments.

We recommended the Liquidating Officer to strictly follow the provisions of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume II as regards the proper maintenance of cashbook to ensure completeness and correctness thereof and to facilitate verification and reconciliation.

We recommended that the Accountable Officer prepare the monthly Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) for checks at the end of each month in compliance with the above-cited regulation.
We recommended that the Accountable Officer continue the proper handling of government funds pursuant to regulations.

We recommended that upon completion of the physical count by the Inventory Committee, the Local Chief Executive should require the GSO to institute measures to properly dispose of all obsolete and unserviceable PPEs pursuant to the directives of Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 379 – 381 of R.A. No. 7160, and in accordance with the procedures cited in COA Circular No. 89 – 296.

We recommended that Management require:
the Supply Officer-designate to determine the employees responsible for the lost/missing items by examining its copies of Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs); and
the accountable officers to file Notice of Loss and Request for Relief from Accountability with the Commission on Audit and attach the supporting documents required by Commission on Audit Memorandum No. 92-751.

We recommended and reiterated the management to require:
the Municipal Engineer to provide the Accountant and the General Service Officer with the complete description and segregation of road components for those with data on construction cost. For those not posted in the registry but without identified cost and those included in the RPCRNS but not in the registry, the fair value must be used in determining the cost of road components and thereafter shall provide the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
the Municipal Accountant to depreciate the carrying amount of existing roads and its components to reflect the asset in its used condition and accordingly adjust the amount recorded in the Road Network account supported by the RPCRNS, Registry Infrastructure for Roads and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost per registry. Subsequently, at the end of each year, a lapsing schedule for the computation of depreciation for each depreciable road component must be prepared as basis for recording depreciation expenses and the required Local Road Network Ledger Card for each road network shall likewise be maintained; and
the designated SO at the end or every accounting period to render a Report on Local Road Network using the pro-forma schedule in Annex A of same Circular and maintain local road inventory, Road Map and complete Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components.

We recommended the management to:
expedite the implementation of all on – going programmed and funded projects under the 20% MDF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the municipality;
continuously ensure that the development projects that are included under the 20% DF shall be necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, and essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the people; and
ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to:
trace the original entry made and make the necessary adjustments on the outstanding payable accounts and ensure that such claims be properly documented and supported with valid claims of speciﬁc creditors, processed vouchers or payrolls, demand payments or bills;
maintain complete and updated subsidiary ledgers for Payable accounts; and
ensure that payable accounts that have been outstanding for more than two years and not supported with documents evidencing valid claims should be reverted to unappropriated surplus of the general fund.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to:
conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs to establish the veracity of account balances in the financial statements; and
maintain subsidiary ledgers per Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and facilitate the monitoring of the existence of funds and the timely implementation thereof.

We recommended that management:
conduct regular analysis on the causes of delay in the implementation of projects and formulate specific, achievable and time-bound measures to ensure timely implementation PPAs; and
continuously monitor and ensure that the funds are used only for the purpose/s for which they were granted.

We recommended that management require the immediate return of all unused balances to source agencies or request authority for the continued use of funds for other identified projects of the municipality.

We recommended and reiterate that management:
through the Municipal Accountant to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
in the future, for transactions involving receivables, the Municipal Accountant should maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

We recommend that management:
continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended:
the Local Chief Executive to enforce the settlement of the above audit disallowances which have become final and executory and in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006;
the Municipal Accountant to properly monitor and reconcile the balance of receivables on disallowances/charges recognized in the financial statements with that contained in the RADC and make the necessary adjustments in consonance with the provisions set forth under COA Circular No. 2009-006; and
the Municipal Treasurer to immediately notify the Audit Team regarding settlement of disallowances and submit proof of settlement thereof for prompt issuance of NSSDC, or in the alternative, submit a monthly summary of settlement of disallowances.

We recommended that management further strengthen its internal control by instructing all concerned to properly accomplish all accounting and procurement forms by strictly following the instructions in the MNGAS for LGUs and procurement laws and regulations to ensure propriety and propriety regularity of its transactions.

We recommended the Management to direct the BAC/BAC Secretariat to prepare and submit the APP, PMR and APCPI Results to the GPPB and comply with the posting requirements as required in R.A. No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 02-2020. Thereafter, please furnish the Office of the Auditor of a copy of each report.

We recommended the Accounting Office to require the submission of the lacking documentary requirements and forward the same to the audit team for evaluation. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should strictly comply with his/her function under Section 474(b)(5) of R.A. No. 7160 to ensure the completeness of supporting documents before payment to contractors/suppliers.
To ensure that all lacking documents are submitted, we recommend that the Accounting Office accomplish the attached updated checklist of documentary requirements provided to management and attach in the disbursement vouchers.

We recommend the BAC/BAC Secretariat to strictly observe Section 37.4 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 and ensure that the Notice to Proceed are issued and received by the successful bidders within seven (7) calendar days from the date of contract approval.

We recommended the BAC/BAC Secretariat to require the contractors/winning bidders to indicate the date of actual receipt on the NOA and NTP in order to determine the proper time of the execution of the contract and the start of its execution, and as basis in computing liquidated damages, in case of delay in its performance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the BAC/BAC Secretariat to immediately submit the documents lacking. Otherwise, we will be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension pursuant to COA rules and regulations.
To ensure that all lacking documents are submitted, we urged the responsible personnel to accomplish the documentary requirements checklist and put index tabs/tabbings on the documents to be submitted.
Henceforth, we recommended Management, thru the Bids and Awards Committee, to ensure that all supporting contract documents are complete in compliance with pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR, and submitted within five (5) days from execution of contracts in accordance with Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the BAC/BAC Secretariat to immediately submit the documents lacking. Otherwise, we will be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension pursuant to COA rules and regulations.
To ensure that all lacking documents are submitted, we urged the responsible personnel to accomplish the documentary requirements checklist and put index tabs/tabbings on the documents to be submitted.
Henceforth, we recommended Management, thru the Bids and Awards Committee, to ensure that all supporting contract documents are complete in compliance with pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR, and submitted within five (5) days from execution of contracts in accordance with Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

The RAAFs submitted by the Treasury Office have now been properly accomplished.
The Disbursing Officer and the Municipal Accountant have reconciled the cashbook and Accounting records to monitor accountabilities effectively.

In the 2022 RPCPPE, there are still unserviceable items totaling ₱2,065,862.07 (gross) which were not subjected to disposal and inclusion of missing items totaling ₱285,048.00 (gross) which were not requested for relief from property accountability.

observations.managementAction: Management has continuously complied with the allocation and utilization of 5% LDRRMF in accordance with pertinent rules and regulations.
All on-going PPAs as of December 31, 2021 were completed and implemented during FY 2022.
Management has observed the guidelines in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances. However, the settlement of the remaining balance of ₱163,903.51 was not yet enforced.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 17, page 59 of this AAR.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer was able to submit the required reports and has faithfully complied with the audit recommendations.

The Municipal Accounting Office is currently conducting analysis of its cash and cash equivalents.                                                    

Management is ensuring that all Trust Funds are used only for their intended purpose.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 6, page 33 of this AAR

observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office has initially prepared adjustments to the affected accounts. However, remaining payables amounting to ₱202,544.67 were still not supported with subsidiary ledgers and supporting documents.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 11, page 46 of this AAR

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has conducted verification of the accounts and was able to reduce it to ₱486,909.54. Moreover, demand letters were already sent to Accountable employees but no settlement has yet been made.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 16, page 56 of this AAR

observations.managementAction: Management complied with the recommendations and has appropriated more than the 5% for GAD PPAs. Also, documents were already made available and trainings were conducted to strengthen their knowledge on HGDG tool.
Management has complied with the regulations set in planning and monitoring the SEF budget and expenditures.
The concerned SB officials already returned the excessive and irregularly disbursed funds.

observations.recommendation: That the City Accountant provide for depreciation and impairment losses of the City’s assets for proper valuation and recording.
observations.managementAction: There is still no clear valuation method and depreciation matrix as basis for asset recognition. According to the City Engineer, Chairperson of the Road Network Committee, they are waiting for COA's action on their pending request for assistance on road valuation.

observations.recommendation: Consider creating an Internal Audit Service independent of the City Accounting Office, to perform staff functions in accordance with the above provisions of Republic Act No. 4177 and Administrative Order Nos. 70 and 278.
observations.managementAction: As per AAPSI dated September 2021, the CHRMO stated they will consider the creation of Internal Audit Service pursuant to Republic Act No. 4177.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended management that upon completion of physical count of the Inventory Committee, the Local Chief Executive require the GSO to institute measures to properly dispose all obsolete and unserviceable PPEs pursuant to the directives of Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 379 – 381 of R.A. No. 7160 and in accordance with the procedures cited in COA Circular No. 89 – 296.

We recommended that management require:

the Supply Officer-designate to determine the employees responsible for the lost/missing items by examining its copies of Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs); and

the accountable officers to file Notice of Loss and Request for Relief from Accountability with the Commission on Audit and attach the supporting documents required by Commission on Audit Memorandum No. 92-751.

We recommended that management require the maintenance of Property Cards to properly account the receipt and disposition of PPE items.

We recommended that management require the disclosure on reconciliation of the carrying amount at the beginning and end of the period.
observations.managementAction: In the 2021 RPCPPE, there are still unserviceable items totaling ₱2,200,600.07 (gross) which were not subjected to disposal and inclusion of missing items totaling ₱276,947.33 (gross) which were not requested for relief from property accountability.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 5, page 33 of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:
The Local Treasurer shall submit to the LCE a certified statement covering the income and expenditures of the preceding fiscal year, the actual income and expenditures of the first two (2) quarters of the current year, and the estimated income and expenditures for the last two (2) quarters of the current year (Section 315, R.A. No. 7160); and

The Local Accountant, jointly with the Local Treasurer, shall certify all statement of income and expenditure of the preceding fiscal year, the actual income and expenditures of the first two (2) quarters of the current year and the estimated income and expenditure for the last two (2) quarters of the current year (Article 411, IRR of R.A. No. 7160).
observations.managementAction: The prescribed form was not yet used in FY 2021.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 10, page 50 of this AAR

observations.recommendation:
1.12. We recommended to the City Government the following:
1.12.1. that the City Treasury Office provide the City Accountant with
the list of all Leaseholders particularly those with delinquencies
and those with advance payments, including the amounts due
such as unpaid rents, penalties, surcharges and interests for the
proper recording and reporting in the books and in the
Financial Statements; and
1.12.2. that the City Accounting Office record as income/debit to
equity, the accumulated uncollected rent income including
penalties, on the lease of market stalls and lease of lots owned
and administered by the City Government from CY 2012 to
2021, totaling ₱41,750,989.45, and recognize the corresponding
receivable accounts of the delinquent leaseholders.
1.12.3. henceforth, recognize and record income from all sources using
the accrual basis of accounting as prescribed under the IPSAS,
and recognize the corresponding appropriate receivable
accounts.

2.9. We recommended the City Government to:
2.9.1. instruct the City Treasury Office to update the records of their
leaseholders to incorporate corrections and proper computations
of the amounts due;
2.9.2. instruct the City Treasury Office to provide the City Accountant
with the list and pertinent records of leaseholders for the proper
and timely recording and reporting of income as well as
penalties, surcharges, interests and other delinquent charges;
and
2.9.3. set standard and clear remedies to be adopted in the settlement
of delinquent leaseholders, including uniform computations of
penalties, surcharges, interests and other delinquent charges.

3.28. In view of the forgoing, we recommended the following:
3.28.1. that the General Services Office expedite the preparation of
pertinent supporting documents, such as the AIR, RIS and
ARE, and immediately forward to the CAO the copies of
thereof;
3.28.2. that the City Accounting Office draw the following journal
entry to adjust the balances of PPE accounts and other affected
accounts:
To recognize the delivered PPE:
ICT Equipment 18,181,825.00
Communication Equipment 2,054,200.00
Technical and Scientific Equipment 133,022.40
Construction and Heavy Equipment 18,500.00
Other Machineries and Equipment 1,031,456.00
Motor Vehicles 10,839,300.00
Accounts Payable 32,258,303.40
To reclassify the asset recorded as Other Prepaid Expense to its
proper account:
ICT Equipment 212,076.80
Other Prepaid Expense 212,076.80
To recognize the asset arising from non-exchange transactions:
General Fund:
ICT Equipment 247,998.00
Construction and Heavy Equipment 380,500.00
Military, Police and Security Equipment 240,467.20
Government Equity 868,965.20
To record transfers of assets to General Fund
ICT Equipment 185,808.92
Medical Equipment 600,000.00
Government Equity (Income) 785,808.92
To record acquired assets from trust accounts
under the General Fund
Motor Vehicle 300,000.00
Government Equity
(Income from Grants and Donations) 300,000.00
To record received donation of 1-unit passenger bus
To recognize depreciation expenses:
Prior Year's Adjustment 340,615.08
Depreciation Expenses 2,691,798.82
Accumulated Depreciation 3,032,413.89
3.28.3. to record in the proper accounts, assets acquired through trust
accounts and from non-exchange transactions, such as transfers
of assets from other agencies.

4.13. We recommended that the City Government:
4.13.1. reconstitute an Inventory Committee, with adequate number of
members, to plan and conduct complete inventory taking of all
the properties of the City Government in an efficient and
effective manner;
4.13.2. undertake the procedural guidelines under COA Circular No.
2020-006 on the inventory and disposition of PPE for the one-
time cleansing of PPE account balances.

5.10. We recommended that the City Government:
5.10.1. recognize and record in the current year books of accounts the
expenditures incurred in the current year;
5.10.2. formulate guidelines and procedures to enhance the proper
disposition and treatment of unpaid obligations and to enhance
the procurement process so that the obligation of transactions
incurred are implemented on the same year.
5.10.3. ensure that obligations are supported with valid claims as
evidenced by signed contracts, POs, MOA or equivalent
documents.

6.17. We recommended that the City Government:
6.17.1. Facilitate the remittance of the unexpended balance of NALGU
funds to the account of the Bureau of Treasury;
6.17.2. Identify the source agencies who provided the funds invested
under LBP T/D No. 3551-0046-69 and LBP T/D No. 3551-0077-
81, with year-end balances of ₱5,548,005.46 and ₱12,139,306.70,
respectively, and accordingly coordinate with the said agencies
for the proper disposition of the unexpended balances. A
request may also be sent to the source agencies for the transfer
of unexpended balances to the General Fund if the disposition
thereof has not been provided in the MOA; and
6.17.3. Return the ₱50,000,000.00 PAGCOR fund and the
₱438,750,000.00 DICT fund to the Trust Fund Current Account
for immediate implementation of the intended projects; and
6.17.4. Henceforth, ensure that only idle funds in the General Fund as
defined under existing rules and regulations are put in money
market placements.

7.12. We recommended that the City Government:
7.12.1. Deposit to the National Treasury the unused and remaining
balance of the LGSF-AC under the General Fund totaling
₱39,847,660.57;
7.12.2. Return to the Source Agencies all remaining
unexpended/unutilized balances of fund transfers received from
NGAs in CY 2019 and earlier or, make a request to the source
agencies for the transfer of unexpended balances to the General
Fund if the disposition thereof has not been provided in the
MOA;
7.12.3. Through the City Accountant, prepare the necessary journal
entry vouchers (JEVs) to liquidate the long outstanding
balances of the accounts under Due to Other NGA’s, supported
with the supporting documents required under section 3.1.2 of
COA Circular 2012-001 such as Copy of MOA/Trust
Agreement, Copy of OR issued by the Source Agency
evidencing refund of unexpended/unutilized balance of fund
transfer, and other necessary supporting documents depending
on the nature of transactions.

8.12. We recommended the following to the City Government:
8.12.1. Facilitate the full implementation of the project funded under
the Due to Other NGAs – Financial Assistance for the 911
Command Center Phase 1 at CDRRMO Building/1 Lot – Smart
City Command and Control Center amounting to
₱200,000,000.00;
8.12.2. Impose Liquidated Damages on the claim of the supplier, Noah
Process and Materials, Inc., for not satisfactorily delivering the
goods/services under the Purchase Order Agreement within the
specified delivery period plus the duly granted time extension;
and
8.12.3. Through the City Accountant, liquidate the fund transfer under
the Due to Other NGAs – Financial Assistance for the 911
Command Center Phase 1 at CDRRMO Building amounting to
₱200,000,000.00 starting with the submission of the documents
required by the Chief Accountant, Office of the President,
Malacañang.

9.7. We recommended that the City Government through:
9.7.1 the City Accounting Office, adjust the fund transfer treated as
expense to its appropriate asset accounts and furnish this office
of the copy of the JEV adjusting the same. Furthermore,
maintain a subsidiary ledger of the cash transferred pertaining
to the procurement of the medicines and eventually, to
monitor/demand the liquidations by BGHMC; and
9.7.2 the HSO to issue an Acknowledgement Receipt for the EDLA
turned over by BGHMC and ensure that the same is properly
reported in the inventory records as well as in the accounting
books of accounts. Further, ensure proper monitoring of the
drugs so as to guaranty that the purpose for which these
important items were procured is strictly observed and complied
with.

10.6. We recommended that the City Government ensure that all its PPAs
funded in the Annual and Supplemental Budgets are fully aligned with
the City’s Approved Annual Investment Plan pursuant to the Budget
Operations Manual for LGUs.

11.6. We recommended that the City Government expedite the
implementation of all the priority development projects as budgeted,
planned and included in the Annual Investment Plan. If possible,
consider augmenting the personnel complement of the pertinent
divisions and offices engaged in the bidding and implementation of
development projects, with the objective of eliminating backlogs in the
implementation of the City Government’s local development projects.

12.5. We recommended that management expedite the implementation of
unrealized PPAs and also consider the realignment/revision of PPAs
that needs to be updated in order to suit the current needs of the
intended beneficiaries.

13.8. In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following:
13.8.1. That the City Government demand immediate payment of all
the due and demandable receivables and henceforth strictly
enforce the loan repayment scheme guidelines for all loans in
order to ensure that the benefits intended to be derived from
the City’s loan-relending program is optimized;
13.8.2. That management exert all available remedies in enforcing the
immediate settlement of the long outstanding receivables. If
warranted, consider filing a request for Authority to Write-off
for the 399 dormant receivable accounts totaling ₱2,561,506.00
following the guidelines and procedures provided in Section 8.2
and 8.3 of COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016;
and
13.8.3. That management make a thorough assessment of its Loans
Receivable accounts and provide adequate Allowance for
Impairment so as to fairly present the actual recoverable
balance as required by the International Public Sector
Accounting Standards.

14.13. In view of the forgoing, we recommended to management the following:
14.13.1. Conduct an evaluation of the specific infrastructure projects,
deliveries and excavation activities with corresponding long
outstanding guaranty deposits payable. If there are no issues
or pending matters that would hinder the release of their
guaranty deposits, facilitate the immediate release of the same
by encouraging the concerned individuals and entities to file
their claims the soonest;
14.13.2. Revert to the General Fund all unclaimed Restoration Bonds
payable with no pending claims or issues to be resolved and
where the required 60-day holding period have already lapsed;
and,
14.13.3. In view of the significant number of parties affected, notify
through publication all the concerned individuals/entities who
have tree cutting bonds that are already up for refund.
Alternatively, in view of the relative immateriality of the
amounts involved per payee, the same may be requested for
automatic reversion to the General Fund, similar to that of the
restoration bonds, by proposing to the City Council the
corresponding amendment to the pertinent ordinance
requiring the said impositions.

15.23. In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management submit
copy of the delegation from the City Mayor authorizing the BAC or the
End-user unit or any other appropriate bureau, committee, support or
procuring unit to directly negotiate with Loakan Laundry Services and
Robinson’s Handyman as required under GPPB Resolution No. 3-2020.
Henceforth, ensure that procedural, legal and documentation
requirements are strictly followed in future procurements.

16.8. We reiterate our recommendation that LGU exhaust all available
remedies provided under the laws, rules and regulations in enforcing
immediate compliance for the settlement of the Charges and for the
Disallowances without pending appeal to COA as provided in the
RRSA.

16.9. We likewise recommended that the City Accounting Office revert the
amount of ₱49,985.00 from Accounts Receivable to Receivable –
Disallowances/Charges account and cause its immediate settlement.

17.11. We recommended that the City Government through the GFPS:
17.11.1. Enhance and further develop the GAD Database consisting of
sex-disaggregated data and other GAD-related information;
and
17.11.2. Submit GAD AR duly reviewed by the DILG with all the
required attachments enumerated in Section 4, C.8. of PCW-
DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Accountable Officer to immediately restitute the cash shortage by depositing the amount to the appropriate bank account or by submitting a sufficient explanation or allowable justification.
Henceforth, the AO/Municipal Treasurer should deposit intact all her collections as well as all collections turned over to her by the collectors with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day. RCDs and cashbooks should also be properly prepared and maintained.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer perform monthly reconciliation of cashbook and accounting records to ensure timely discovery of errors and to facilitate early preparation of applicable adjustments.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure complete and proper accomplishment of the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms to ensure accuracy thereof and to promote accountability.

We recommended that Management through the Disbursing Officer and the Municipal Accountant reconcile their cashbook and accounting records, respectively, at least quarterly in compliance to Section 96 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that upon completion of the physical count by the Inventory Committee, the Local Chief Executive should require the GSO to institute measures to properly dispose of all obsolete and unserviceable PPEs pursuant to the directives of Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 379 – 381 of R.A. No. 7160, and in accordance with the procedures cited in COA Circular No. 89 – 296.
We recommended that Management require:

the Supply Officer-designate to determine the employees responsible for the lost/missing items by examining its copies of Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs); and
the accountable officers to file Notice of Loss and Request for Relief from Accountability with the Commission on Audit and attach the supporting documents required by Commission on Audit Memorandum No. 92-751.

We recommended that Management require the maintenance of Property Cards to properly account the receipt and disposition of PPE items.

We recommended that management:

continuously ensure the allocation and utilization of LDRRMF pursuant to the policies and guidelines primarily embodied in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1;
thru the LDRRMC, continuously monitor, coordinate and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of the most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible projects; and
continuously comply with pertinent laws, rules and regulations in the recording, utilization and reporting of LDRRMF.

We recommended that management:

expedite the implementation of all ongoing programmed and funded projects under the 20% MDF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the Municipality;
continuously monitor, coordinate, and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of the most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible development projects; and
continuously observe the policies and guidelines in the appropriation and utilization of the 20% DF as enjoined by DBM-DOF-DILG-JMC No. 1, S. 2020.

We commended management for its remarkable effort in enforcing the liquidation of substantial amount of cash advances. Nevertheless, we recommended that management:

continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that in the budget preparation phase:
The Municipal Treasurer shall submit to the LCE a certified statement covering the income and expenditures of the preceding fiscal year, the actual income and expenditures of the first two (2) quarters of the current year, and the estimated income and expenditures for the last two (2) quarters of the current year (Section 315, R.A. No. 7160); and
The Municipal Accountant, jointly with the Municipal Treasurer, shall certify all statement of income and expenditure of the preceding fiscal year, the actual income and expenditures of the first two (2) quarters of the current year and the estimated income and expenditure for the last two (2) quarters of the current year (Article 411, IRR of R.A. No. 7160).

We recommended and reiterated that management:

through the Municipal Accountant to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
in the future, for transactions involving receivables, the Municipal Accountant should maintain subsidiary ledgers following Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

We recommended that the Local School Board comply with the procedural guidelines set in DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 to ensure proper planning and monitoring of SEF budget and expenditures.

We recommended that the concerned Municipal Officials/SB members return the excessive and irregularly disbursed funds, otherwise, a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued. Further, management should observe the guidelines set by DBM LBC No. 103 in computing the amount of RATA to be paid to prevent irregular disbursements of government funds.

a. Immediately request Squidpay to incorporate a Front-End system that communicates through user interface or standard communication protocols that collects the data required and automatically issue an Electronic Official Receipt (EOR) and to institute some urgent measures designed to mitigate the adverse effects of the noted deficiencies. Meantime, temporary discontinuance of the use of the Electronic Payment Collection System provided by the Electronic Payment Gateway Provider - Squidpay Technology, Inc. until such time that Squidpay organizes its system is also recommended; and
b. Require Squidpay Technology, Inc. to assist the City Treasurer in resolving the issue on the discrepancy of collections in the market arrival fees.

It appears that Squidpay over-credited P43,248.00 to the Squidpay Account of the City. Several tests have been done and the new - system proved to be successful.
